celebration: The magic-e wand returns — this week it lands on the letter i. The class now owns two magic-e vowels (a_e, i_e): touch the red e to bit and it becomes bite, to kit and it becomes kite. The i says its NAME.

songs.title: Magic E Makes Bike
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(eye… eye… eye…)
Magic E! Magic E!

[Hook]
Bit… BITE! Kit… KITE!
Magic E! Magic E!
Fin… FINE! Pin… PINE!
The E is magic! (shhh — it's silent!)

[Verse 2]
Hid… HIDE! Rid… RIDE!
Magic E! Magic E!
The I says its NAME! (I! I!)
And the E says… (nothing!)

[Verse 3]
B-I-K-E — bike!
K-I-T-E — kite!
F-I-V-E — five!
(Count them! Count them!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! No magic E!
(eye… eye… eye…)

[Outro]
The E is magic.
The E is silent. (shhh…)
BIKE!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: The transformation pairs ARE the hook — 808 hit exactly on each change (bit→BITE). The '(eye… eye… eye…)' stutters sing the LONG I sound /aɪ/ (phonetic respelling rule: sung 'eye', printed i_e). Spell-outs are uppercase letter names (B-I-K-E) per the whole-words rule. 'The I says its NAME' is the whole lesson in one line; the silent-e whisper gag lands the point — the e works without making a sound. Second week of the Magic E series (cake→bike).

songs.title: The Bike Ride
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
Sheep is on the bike!
Sheep is on the bike!
Ride, Sheep, ride! (ride! ride!)
Sheep is on the bike!

[Verse 2]
A kite is up! (up! up!)
Snake has the kite!
Ride, Sheep, ride! (ride! ride!)
The kite can ride!

[Verse 3]
The bike is quick! (quick! quick!)
It is time to ride! (go! go!)
Ride, Sheep, ride! (ride! ride!)
Sheep can ride the bike!

[Outro]
The bike… the kite… (shhh…)
On the bike?! (a potato!)
(POTATO!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'Sheep is on the bike.' — every content word is magic-e or Level-1 known. Verse 2 flies the kite behind the bike; verse 3 is the ride climax. The 'Ride, Sheep, ride!' call carries the Level-1 command-frame ('Sit, cat, sit!') forward. Whisper the outro, then the potato hits loud.

spreads.artNote: A gleaming little bike alone in the spotlight, one pedal caught mid-turn. The children know this stage.
The bike beside a big round clock reading five — it is time to go. Anticipation.
Sheep — the fluffy round white sheep with kind dark eyes — perched happily on the saddle, hooves on the pedals.
Sheep pedalling fast, wool streaming back, wheels a blur. Pure joy.
A bright diamond kite with a long ribbon tail, hovering in the spotlight.
Snake — the friendly emerald-green garden snake with large gentle amber eyes — holding the kite string in its tail, delighted.
The kite climbs high above the dark trees, string taut, tail rippling.
Snake ties the kite string to the back of Sheep's bike — the kite rides ALONG behind them as they zoom. Teamwork triumph.
The bike is parked — and a single potato sits proudly on the saddle wearing a tiny bike helmet. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it. Sheep and Snake peek from the edge of frame.

sentences: It is time to ride the bike.
extras: THE MAGIC-E WAND, week 2: build bit with the moveable alphabet, then touch the red wooden e to the end — bit→bite, kit→kite, rid→ride, fin→fine, pin→pine, hid→hide. The i changes its voice from /i/ to /aɪ/. Same wand, new vowel — the class already knows the trick from a_e.
Pattern-card tracing continues: pattern letters in red, frame word in black, the silent e greyed with a halo — trace the word, whisper nothing at the e.
Minimal-pair sort (short i vs long i): bit/bite, kit/kite, rid/ride, pin/pine, fin/fine, dim/dime — hear the vowel flip.
Moveable alphabet with the special red e: b-i-k-e, k-i-t-e, t-i-m-e, f-i-v-e.

teacherFocus: MAGIC-E, WEEK 2 — the letter i. The wand is the whole presentation: build bit, touch the red e, and the i changes its voice — bit→bite, kit→kite, rid→ride, fin→fine, pin→pine, hid→hide. Do all six pairs before any new words. The children already know the trick from a_e last week, so this is consolidation, not a new mechanic — but the minimal pairs (bit/bite) are the sharpest hearing test yet. Pattern-card tracing continues: the silent e stays grey with a halo. Sheep and Snake return to star.
